competitors.label: Render and Commit
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical React explanation of render work, commit work, and why a rerender does not equal a DOM mutation.
competitors.winReason: The official docs cover the pipeline, but our page is more explicit about real rerender root causes: unstable identities, context fanout, remounts from key/type changes, and StrictMode dev confusion.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative explanation of render and commit phases.
theirGaps: It is less focused on rerender root-cause debugging.

realWorldUseCases: many rerender bugs are really identity bugs, context fanout, or remount problems
followUpConfusion: Changing a component’s key or swapping wrappers (e.g., Fragment ↔ div) can cause a remount (state reset), not just a re-render.

realWorldUseCases: The docs explain how rendering and commit work, but they do not spend as much time on identity bugs, remounts, and context blast radius.
actionableExamples: They still provide concrete pipeline examples, so the example criterion is close.
followUpConfusion: Our page is stronger on the follow-up distinction between rerender, remount, and StrictMode-only double logs.

actionableExamples: Add one concrete example where a key change remounts a subtree and wipes local form state.

competitors.label: What does re-rendering mean in React?
competitors.selectionReason: This is the closest GreatFrontEnd interview-prep page for rerender semantics and render-cycle follow-ups.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd defines rerendering well, but our page is more complete on bailout preconditions, StrictMode double logs, and key/type changes causing remounts instead of plain rerenders.
theirStrengths: It is concise, interview-shaped, and directly relevant to rerender terminology.
theirGaps: It is less explicit about remounts, unstable identities, and context-driven rerenders.

realWorldUseCases: Unnecessary re-renders usually come from unclear state ownership, unstable prop identities (inline objects/functions), broad context updates, or bad keys.
followUpConfusion: StrictMode (dev): React may intentionally run render logic more than once to surface unsafe side effects.

realWorldUseCases: The quiz explains rerendering broadly, but our page names more concrete production causes of unexpected rerendering.
actionableExamples: Both pages provide interview-scale explanations, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: Our page is clearer about rerender versus remount and about why key changes reset state.

actionableExamples: Add a short context-provider example that rerenders many consumers after one value change.

competitors.label: React re-renders guide
competitors.selectionReason: DeveloperWay is the strongest long-form tutorial on rerender causes, memoization, and prop identity for React.
theirStrengths: It provides a broader catalog of rerender causes and optimization patterns.
theirGaps: It is less compressed for quick interview delivery.

realWorldUseCases: Before/after bug: memoized child still hot because prop identity changes
actionableExamples: const filters = React.useMemo(() => ({ region, includeArchived: false }), [region]);

realWorldUseCases: The guide is explicitly organized around real rerender triggers like parent rerenders, props identity churn, and memo boundaries.
actionableExamples: It contains more worked examples and deeper rerender taxonomies than our current answer.
followUpConfusion: Both pages separate rerendering from DOM mutation and discuss memoization, so the follow-up criterion stays close.

realWorldUseCases: Add a second real bug example around inline objects and callbacks breaking memoized children.
actionableExamples: Add one small before-and-after memo example with unstable versus stable props.
followUpConfusion: Keep one extra line on how key changes cause identity loss even when DOM output looks similar.

competitors.winReason: Our page now adds a second unstable-prop example and a remount-by-key snippet, which moves it ahead on production use-cases and actionable examples while conceptual depth stays close.
